title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Introduction -- Influential Factors and Variables for Construct Stiffness and Stability
content: One method of changing the stability is to alter the pin configuration. Placing pins closer to the fracture site, adding more pins and increasing the spread of the pins will all add to the stiffness of the construct. However, one must also place the pins out of the field of future surgical approaches during definitive fixation. Any increases in pin diameter will strengthen the construct to the fourth power and reduce the stress at the bone-pin interface. Increasing pin diameter has the greatest influence on the stability of unilateral frames. That said, larger pins increase the risk of a potential stress riser and can ultimately lead to fracture. [1] For example; a 5 mm pin is 144% stiffer than a 4 mm pin.

title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Introduction -- External Fixator Types
content: There are many different ways to change and enhance the external fixation construct. To complicate things further, there are also hybrid frames which are a combination of any of the previous constructs described. The surgeon must create a level of stability that is appropriate for optimal healing. It is essential also to have a good understanding of basic fracture principals because stiffer is not always better when it comes to external fixation.

title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Contraindications
content: External fixation is a relatively safe minimally invasive procedure and can provide significant benefit to the patient when used in the correct setting. As such, there are limited contraindications for its use in orthopedics. Relative contraindications include an obese patient where safely placing pins would be difficult. A non-compliant patient is a relative contraindication because he or she may not follow up for the removal of the device. Also, peri-prosthetic fractures can limit the bone stock available to place the pins. General contraindications include patient refusal, unable to withstand the procedure physiologically.

title: Ankle Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Open reduction and internal fixation
content: Posterior malleolus fractures can be addressed with a posteroanterior lag screw or an antiglide plate. Intraoperative assessment of the ankle syndesmosis is essential and can be performed using the Hook test (Cotton test) or intraoperative stress external rotation views to confirm proper alignment and stability. [20] [21]  The Hook test can be performed by the lateral distraction of the fibula with a bone hook under the image intensifier screen. The Hook test is considered positive if there is more than 2 mm of displacement of the fibula. [22]  If the hook test is positive and there is a syndesmosis diastasis, the syndesmosis should be reduced by applying a big reduction clamp to the medial and lateral malleoli and then fixed either by syndesmotic screws or tightropes.

title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Introduction -- External Fixator Types
content: External fixator types divide into several different subcategories, including uniplanar, multiplanar, unilateral, bilateral, and circular fixators. By adding pins in different planes (i.e., placed perpendicular to each other), one can create a multiplanar construct. Uniplanar fixation devices are fast and easy to apply but are not as sturdy as multiplanar fixation. Bilateral frames are created when the pins are on both sides of the bone and can also add additional stability. Circular fixators have gained popularity with limb lengthening procedures but are especially effective at allowing the patient to weight bear and maintain some joint motion during the treatment. They are more difficult to apply and use smaller gauge pins and more of them to distribute the weight. [3]

title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Introduction
content: Physicians have been using external fixation to treat fractures for more than 2000 years after being first described by Hippocrates as a way to immobilize the fracture while preserving soft tissue integrity. The fixator design and biomechanics have changed dramatically over the years, but the principles remain the same. The primary goal of external fixation is to maintain the length, alignment, and rotation of the fracture. External fixation can serve as provisional fixation or definitive fixation purposes. Both methods can be performed in conjunction with partial internal fixation if necessary. It is important for orthopedic surgeons at a trauma center to be familiar with the techniques and principles of external fixation for various fractures of the upper extremity, lower extremity, and pelvis.

title: Distal Femur Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Open Reduction Internal Fixation
content: The 95-degree blade plate is a useful implant for fracture fixation of OTA 33 type A patterns, as well as for the stabilization of malunions and nonunions.  The placement of the blade plate is often a technically demanding procedure because three planes must simultaneously be corrected. Condylar malalignment may result if the chisel and plate are incorrectly inserted. Sliding barrel condylar plates are devices based on the compression screw used for hip fractures. These devices allow for the ability to apply compression across the femoral condyles. They allow better purchase in osteoporotic bone and only need two planes of correction when compared to the blade plate. Potential disadvantages with this construct include its bulky size and the requirement to remove a large amount of bone from the lateral femoral condyle. Due to its bulky size, the plate may cause symptomatic hardware and knee symptoms. Clinical studies have generally shown good results with fixed angled blade plate and sliding barrel condylar plate devices with 82% reporting satisfactory to excellent results at 9.5 years for condylar plate and 81% good or excellent results for the dynamic condylar screw. [20] [21] A recent study comparing the less invasive stabilization system (LISS) with minimally invasive sliding barrel condylar plates in the treatment of OTA 33 type A1 to C2 concluded that there was no advantage for locking plate designs. [22]

title: Forearm Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Methods of Fixation
content: Dynamic compression and limited contact dynamic compression are the most commonly used techniques for fixation of the forearm fractures. These are mostly used for simple fracture patterns, especially the transverse and short oblique types. They provide absolute stability at the fracture site and promote primary healing. A lag screw with a neutralization plate is used for oblique fractures. If the fracture is severely comminuted, it should be fixed by bridge plating mode. The intramedullary nail is a load-sharing device that can also be used for the fixation of forearm fractures. The advantages of nailing are preserving the fracture hematoma (closed reduction) and periosteal blood supply in the forearm. [6]

title: Forearm Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Open Reduction with Plate and Screw Fixation
content: In both-bone forearm fractures, the order of fixation for the radius and ulna is based on fracture pattern and comminution. Initial fixation of the fracture with less comminution will restore the length and facilitates reduction of the other bone. When both the fractures are simple, preferably radius should be fixed first as it makes the forearm more stable and makes it easy to address the ulna. Placement of the plate is usually preferred on the volar surface. It provides proper seating of the plate and adequate soft tissue coverage; however, placement of the plate on the dorsal (tension) surface is biomechanically better. [6]

content: Nondisplaced fractures are treated with cancellous lag screws or a sliding hip screw. Both techniques have about the same outcomes and complications. However, cancellous lag screws have a higher revision rate than sliding hip screws due to prominent lag screw head irritation of soft tissue as the naturally femoral neck shortens postoperatively. title: Forearm Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Limited Contact Dynamic Compression Plate
content: Perren et al. reported the use of limited contact dynamic compression plate (LCDCP) in 1990. The fixation principle of LCDCP is very similar to the DCP. The advantages over the DCP are 1) decreased periosteal compression and damage, 2) preserves blood supply to the bone, 3) wavy surface provides an increased area for bone graft application, 4) altering the load of the plate delivers pure tension on the plate, and 5) avoids implant-related stress riser and thereby re-fracture after implant removal. [23] [24]

title: Pathologic Fractures -- Surgical Oncology
content: Surgical intervention for a pathologic fracture should only be considered following an exhaustive oncological work-up and biopsy of the lesion. General principles of internal fixation are applicable but may differ when a fracture of pathologic nature is involved. For instance, implant selection should generally adhere to the following guidelines: Load-sharing as opposed to load-bearing when possible Durable implants with the goal of lasting the length of patient survival with disease progression Immediate stability allowing for immediate weight-bearing Length of implant should bypass the lesion by two cortical diameters Cement augmentation when necessary

title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
content: Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is classified as a treatment modality for several specific orthopedic injuries. It is considered a type of treatment for fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur, for fractures or dislocations of the lower extremity excluding the hip and femur, and for fractures or dislocations of the radius and ulna. In each of these contexts, the procedure involves surgically stabilizing the bone without performing a reduction of the fracture, thereby providing a consistent therapeutic approach across multiple anatomical sites.

title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 950
content: Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is a specific surgical treatment used for managing fractures or dislocations of several long bones, including the hip and femur as well as the radius and ulna. It is classified as a particular form of internal fixation of a fracture (تثبيت داخلي للكسر), indicating that it belongs to the broader category of internal fixation procedures performed without prior fracture reduction. Additionally, this procedure is considered a subtype of internal fixation techniques applied to the clavicle without fracture reduction, reflecting its inclusion within the general family of bone‑specific internal fixation methods. Together, these relationships highlight that internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is a targeted operative approach that fits within larger treatment categories for various skeletal injuries, emphasizing its role in orthopedic care for multiple anatomical sites.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 823
content: Osteoporosis/low bone mass (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ)) is a condition marked by diminished bone density that predisposes patients to skeletal fragility. Internal fixation of fractures (تثبيت داخلي للكسر), hip head replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ), and the occurrence of multiple fractures (كسور متعددة) are all recognized as clinical entities that belong to the osteoporosis/low bone mass category. These procedures and fracture patterns commonly arise because weakened bone is less able to withstand normal stresses, necessitating surgical stabilization or joint replacement. Effective management of osteoporosis/low bone mass therefore aims to strengthen bone, reduce the incidence of such fractures, and limit the need for invasive orthopedic interventions.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 771
content: Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is classified as a treatment for both fracture or dislocation of the hip and femur and for fracture or dislocation of the lower extremity other than the hip or femur. This procedure is closely related to the Arabic‑language concept تثبيت داخلي للكسر (internal fixation of fracture), which is considered the inverse of the English term, indicating that they represent reciprocal perspectives on the same surgical approach. Moreover, Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is a specific form of Internal fixation of clavicle without fracture reduction, highlighting its applicability to clavicular injuries when reduction is not performed. Both تثبيت داخلي للكسر and إعاضة رأس الفخذ (hip head replacement) are listed as members of the SMQ category هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (osteoporosis/low bone mass), underscoring the clinical relevance of these surgical interventions in patients with compromised bone quality. Together, these relationships illustrate how internal fixation procedures, including those without fracture reduction, fit within broader treatment categories for lower‑extremity injuries and are particularly pertinent to the management of osteoporotic bone conditions.

title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1085

This procedure, together with other fracture‑related interventions such as hip‑head prosthetic replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) and the broader category of “other fractures,” is classified within the osteoporosis/low bone mass syndrome (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام SMQ). Internal fixation of fractures (تثبيت داخلي للكسر) is a member of the osteoporosis/low bone mass category, reflecting that patients with reduced bone density often require surgical stabilization of broken bones. title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

Osteoplastic techniques—osteoplasty of the femur, femoral lengthening, shortening, and combined lengthening‑shortening with segment transfer—address limb‑length discrepancies and deformities. Growth‑modulating strategies comprise epiphysiodesis of the distal femur (and proximal tibia or fibula) and hemiepiphyseal arrest of the distal femur, proximal tibia, or fibula for correction of genu varus or valgus.
